description: This Figure displays mechanical property characterization of low alloy ultrahigh strength steels (0Al and 1.3Al compositions) designed with high-density NiAl clusters. The primary panel presents engineering stress-strain curves comparing as-quenched (WQ) and tempered (180°C/2h) conditions, demonstrating that the 1.3Al steel achieves an ultimate tensile strength of ~1876 MPa with 13.7% elongation, while maintaining a yield strength of ~1272 MPa. The data reveals that introducing 1.3 wt% Al enables superior toughening: impact toughness reaches 80 J (KU2) versus 65 J for the 0Al counterpart, and fracture toughness improves to 108.0 MPa·m^{1/2} from 97.6 MPa·m^{1/2}. The figure supports the core conclusion that ultrafine B2-NiAl clusters (0.57 nm radius, density ~4.88×10^{24} m^{-3}), co-precipitated with ε-carbides in the tempered 1.3Al steel, facilitate uniform dislocation distribution and delay crack initiation, achieving an exceptional strength-toughness combination that significantly exceeds commercial 300M steel performance.

This figure presents the mechanical properties of two low-alloy ultrahigh-strength steels (0Al and 1.3Al, differing in aluminum content) in the as-quenched (WQ) and tempered conditions (180°C for 2 h). Panel (a) displays engineering stress-strain curves: in the as-quenched state, the 0Al steel exhibits an ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of ~2294 MPa with 9.5% total elongation (TE), while the 1.3Al steel shows ~2145 MPa UTS but fractures prematurely at ~4.3% strain before reaching maximum strength. After tempering, both steels show reduced UTS (~1925 MPa for 0Al and ~1876 MPa for 1.3Al) but significantly improved ductility (TE increases to 13.5% and 13.7%, respectively). Panel (b) shows Charpy U-notch impact absorbing energy (KU₂): the 0Al steel improves from 33 ± 6 J to 65 ± 3 J after tempering, whereas the 1.3Al steel demonstrates a dramatic toughness enhancement from 23 ± 2 J to 80 ± 3 J (approximately 3.5-fold increase), achieving superior impact resistance compared to the 0Al counterpart at comparable strength levels (~1.9 GPa). These results collectively demonstrate that the introduction of high-density NiAl clusters through tempering enables exceptional strength-toughness synergy in the 1.3Al steel.

Figure 2 presents the microstructural characterization of tempered low alloy ultrahigh strength steels. Panel (a) shows a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of the 1.3Al steel revealing a tempered martensitic structure characterized by lath martensite morphology, with an inset displaying higher magnification transmission electron microscopy (TEM) details of the substructure at 0.2 μm scale. Panel (b) displays X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns comparing 0Al and 1.3Al steels, identifying body-centered cubic (bcc) martensite (α) peaks including (110), (200), (211), and (220), and face-centered cubic (fcc) retained austenite (γ) peaks including (111) and (200). The XRD analysis indicates retained austenite contents of 1.0% for 0Al steel and 1.8% for 1.3Al steel. Dislocation densities calculated using the Williamson-Hall method from the broadening of martensitic peaks are ~2.01×10^15 m^-2 for 0Al steel and ~2.20×10^15 m^-2 for 1.3Al steel, showing a slight increase upon Al addition. Both steels were subjected to solution treatment at 970°C for 1 h followed by water quenching and tempering at 180°C for 2 h.

This figure presents transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization of tempered martensitic microstructures in two low-alloy ultrahigh strength steels after heat treatment at 180°C for 2 hours: (a-c) 0Al steel and (d-f) 1.3Al steel. Subfigures (a) and (d) compare the lath martensite morphology at 0.3μm scale, where 0Al steel exhibits sharp lath boundaries while 1.3Al steel displays high-density dislocations (~2.20×10^15 m^-2). Subfigures (b) and (e) reveal thin films of retained austenite (1.0% and 1.8% volume fraction respectively) located at lath boundaries, with selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns confirming the Kurdjumov-Sachs orientation relationship between BCC martensite and FCC austenite. Subfigures (c) and (f) at 100nm scale display nano-sized ε-carbide precipitates (length 100–500 nm, width 3–10 nm in 0Al; thickness ~2.2 nm in 1.3Al) uniformly distributed within laths and aligned along specific crystallographic directions, with SAED patterns verifying the hexagonal close-packed structure. These microstructural features elucidate the precipitation behavior and dislocation substructures that contribute to the strength-toughness synergy, particularly the role of high-density NiAl clusters in 1.3Al steel as reported in the accompanying text.

This figure presents a multiscale characterization of the hierarchical martensitic microstructures in two experimental low-alloy ultrahigh-strength steels (0Al and 1.3Al). The four columns display decreasing structural scales from left to right: (a) EBSD inverse pole figure maps showing prior austenite grains with scale bars of 20 μm; (b) SEM micrographs revealing martensitic packets with scale bars of 10 μm and annotated packet sizes; (c) EBSD orientation maps displaying martensitic blocks with scale bars of 5 μm and marked block widths; and (d) TEM images illustrating martensitic laths with scale bars of 0.2 μm and indicated lath widths. The comparison between 0Al (0.03 wt.% Al) and 1.3Al (1.30 wt.% Al) steels reveals statistically comparable martensitic unit dimensions across all length scales—prior austenite grain sizes of 26.1 ± 9.4 μm (0Al) versus 23.4 ± 5.9 μm (1.3Al), packet sizes of approximately 3.4–3.5 μm, block widths of approximately 0.8–0.9 μm, and lath widths of approximately 0.23–0.24 μm. These quantitative measurements, derived from statistical sampling of approximately 300 prior austenite grains, 200 packets, 200 blocks, and 100 lath widths, demonstrate that differences in martensitic unit sizes are not the primary factor governing the fracture toughness (KIC) and impact energy (KU2) between the two alloys. Instead, the figure supports the core scientific conclusion that the superior toughness in the 1.3Al steel originates from distinct precipitation behavior involving high-density NiAl clusters rather than from microstructural refinement at the martensitic hierarchy level.

This figure presents multiscale characterization of nanoprecipitates in 1.3Al steel tempered at 180°C for 2 h, combining atom probe tomography (APT) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). Panels (a) and (d) show APT isoconcentration surfaces: (a) reveals needle-like ε-carbides (5 at.% C, brown) with ~2.2 nm thickness and ~10 nm spacing, while (d) displays uniformly distributed NiAl clusters (10 at.% Ni + Al, green) with an equivalent radius of 0.57 nm and number density of 4.88 × 10²⁴ m⁻³. Panels (b) and (e) provide HRTEM images with corresponding FFT patterns: (b) confirms ε-carbides possess hcp structure along [110] zone axis within the bcc matrix, and (e) identifies B2-NiAl clusters with bcc structure along [001] zone axis, fully coherent with the matrix. Panels (c) and (f) are proximity histograms showing compositional profiles: (c) indicates ε-carbide cores contain ~8.91 at.% C with minimal other element enrichment, while (f) reveals NiAl cluster centers reach ~13 at.% Ni + Al (sub-stoichiometric), accompanied by slight segregation of Si, Cr, and Mo. The figure supports that ε-carbides and NiAl clusters nucleate independently without competitive partitioning, with NiAl clusters existing as early-stage coherent precipitates.

Atom Probe Tomography (APT) characterization of the 1.3Al steel tempered at 180°C for 2 h, revealing three-dimensional elemental distributions and compositional gradients around ε-carbides and NiAl clusters. Panel (A) displays the atomic distribution of Ni, Al, and C across an ε-carbide cross-section, showing carbon-rich cores (~8.91 at.%) with slight Ni/Al enrichment at the center. Panel (B) presents a proximity histogram from matrix-dispersed NiAl clusters, illustrating the compositional transition where Ni and Al contents increase significantly within the clusters while the surrounding matrix maintains a low carbon concentration (~1.0 at.%), indicating independent nucleation of ε-carbides and NiAl clusters without competitive partitioning. Panel (C) shows 4 at.% Cr+Mo and 7 at.% Si isoconcentration surfaces alongside a proximity histogram of segregated NiAl clusters, revealing co-segregation of Si (enriching from ~4.21 to ~11.21 at.%), Cr, and Mo at banded zones associated with non-dislocation intragranular defects, which provide heterogeneous nucleation sites facilitating NiAl precipitation.

This figure presents Charpy impact test results comparing the toughness of two low alloy ultrahigh strength steels: 0Al steel (baseline) and 1.3Al steel (containing high-density NiAl clusters). Subfigure (a) displays load-deflection curves for both steels, identifying the yield load (PGY, squares) and maximum load (PM, circles). The 0Al steel reaches PM at 2.06 mm deflection, while 1.3Al steel reaches PM at 2.55 mm, indicating enhanced plastic deformation capability. Subfigures (b) and (c) provide integrated load-energy-displacement curves for 0Al and 1.3Al steels, respectively. For 0Al steel: peak load Pm = 39.7 kN, crack initiation energy Ei = 45.0 J (yellow shaded area), crack propagation energy Ep = 17.4 J (purple shaded area), and total impact energy KU2 = 62.4 J. For 1.3Al steel: Pm = 38.6 kN, Ei = 62.0 J, Ep = 21.0 J, and KU2 = 83.0 J. The data demonstrates that 1.3Al steel exhibits 37.8% higher crack initiation energy and 20.7% higher crack propagation energy than 0Al steel. This significant toughening effect is attributed to the uniformly dispersed coherent NiAl clusters (equivalent radius ~0.57 nm, number density ~4.88×10^24 m^-3) which facilitate homogeneous plastic deformation, suppress localized stress concentrations, and promote crack deflection through void coalescence and secondary cracking mechanisms, thereby increasing the energy threshold for crack initiation and extending the crack propagation path.

This figure presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) fractographs of Charpy impact-tested low alloy ultrahigh strength steels, comparing 0Al (baseline) and 1.3Al (NiAl cluster-containing) steels tempered at 180°C for 2 hours. Subfigures (a) and (d) show macroscopic fracture surfaces at 1 mm scale for 0Al and 1.3Al steels respectively, with arrows indicating regions examined at higher magnification. Subfigures (b) and (c) reveal microscopic features of the 0Al steel at 100 µm and 25 µm scales, exhibiting predominant quasi-cleavage fracture with flat facets and limited ductile dimples (highlighted by yellow and blue dashed circles). Conversely, subfigures (e) and (f) display the 1.3Al steel fracture surface characterized by extensive dimple growth (labeled "Dimples growing"), prominent tearing ridges, and significantly higher surface roughness. The morphological transition from quasi-cleavage to ductile dimple rupture indicates that high-density NiAl clusters facilitate void nucleation and coalescence, dissipating impact energy and increasing toughness from 65 J to 80 J (KU2), consistent with the superior plastic deformation capacity reported in the accompanying text.

This figure presents cross-sectional EBSD characterization of impact fracture features in low alloy ultrahigh strength steels comparing 0Al and 1.3Al steels tempered at 180°C for 2 h. Panels (a) and (c) display band contrast (BC) + inverse pole figure (IPF) + grain boundary (GB) maps near main cracks, revealing the martensitic microstructure morphology and crack paths. Panels (b) and (d) are magnified kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps corresponding to regions marked in (a) and (c), respectively, with scales from 0° to 3.5°. The 0Al steel exhibits an average KAM value of 1.16° with inhomogeneous strain distribution dominated by red/yellow high-strain regions near the main crack and significant blue undeformed areas in the adjacent matrix. In contrast, the 1.3Al steel shows an average KAM value of 1.31° with relatively uniform green/yellow distribution across both the fracture surface and surrounding matrix. This uniform strain distribution facilitates effective stress transfer to the interior matrix during fracture, enabling coordinated deformation and mitigating stress concentration. The results support the conclusion that high-density NiAl clusters promote homogeneous dislocation structures through cooperative interactions with dislocations, suppressing pile-ups and local stress concentrations, thereby enhancing fracture toughness.

This figure presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of fracture vertical-section microstructures from fracture toughness specimens of low alloy ultrahigh strength steels, comparing 0Al steel (panels a and c) and 1.3Al steel (panels b and d). Panels (a) and (b) at 50 μm scale display the main crack paths traced by cyan arrows, revealing that the 0Al steel exhibits relatively straight crack propagation with minimal deflections, while the 1.3Al steel shows frequent crack deflections and tortuous paths. Panels (c) and (d) are magnified views (10 μm scale) of the regions marked by blue boxes in (a) and (b), respectively. The 0Al steel (c) displays tip passivation where the main crack occasionally deflects but rapidly blunts upon penetrating the matrix. In contrast, the 1.3Al steel (d) exhibits abundant secondary cracks and micro-voids measuring 5–10 μm located 8–18 μm beneath the main fracture surface, with fine cracks interconnecting these voids indicating coalescence into larger secondary cracks. These observations demonstrate that the 1.3Al steel achieves superior toughness through crack deflection, void nucleation, and coalescence mechanisms induced by high-density NiAl clusters, increasing the crack propagation distance and promoting greater energy dissipation compared to the 0Al counterpart.
